annotations.label: Bijection

anchor.snippet: a bijection , bijective function , or one-to-one correspondence is a function between two sets such that each element of the second set

annotations.note: `Function.Bijective f` is defined as `Injective f ∧ Surjective f`.

annotations.label: Bijective iff invertible

anchor.snippet: A function is bijective if and only if it is invertible

annotations.note: States `Bijective f ↔ ∃ g, LeftInverse g f ∧ RightInverse g f`.

annotations.label: Multiplication by two

anchor.snippet: the multiplication by two defines a bijection from the integers to the even numbers

annotations.note: No specific bijection ℤ ≃ {even integers} via x ↦ 2x is named in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bijective iff injective and surjective

anchor.snippet: A function is bijective if and only if it is both injective

annotations.note: The definition `Bijective f := Injective f ∧ Surjective f` makes this equivalence definitional.

annotations.label: Counting establishes a bijection

anchor.snippet: The elementary operation of counting establishes a bijection from some finite set to the first natural numbers

annotations.note: Every finite type has an equivalence `α ≃ Fin (card α)`.

annotations.label: Permutation

anchor.snippet: A bijective function from a set to itself is also called a permutation

annotations.note: `Equiv.Perm α` is the abbreviation `α ≃ α`.

annotations.label: Bijection (four properties)

anchor.snippet: For a binary relation pairing elements of set X with elements of set Y to be a bijection, four properties must hold

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Function.Bijective` formalizes the same notion (functionality + totality are built into `α → β`).

annotations.label: Surjection and injection

anchor.snippet: Functions which satisfy property (3) are said to be " onto Y " and are called surjections

annotations.note: Both `Function.Surjective` and `Function.Injective` are defined in this file.

annotations.label: Empty set bijection

anchor.snippet: Every map from the empty set to itself is a bijection.

annotations.note: Mathlib has equivalences between any two empty types but no dedicated lemma stating `∅ → ∅` is bijective.

annotations.label: Baseball batting line-up

anchor.section: Batting line-up of a baseball or cricket team
anchor.snippet: Consider the batting line-up of a baseball or cricket team

annotations.note: Informal real-world illustration, not a mathematical statement.

annotations.label: Seats and students

anchor.section: Seats and students of a classroom
anchor.snippet: the instructor declares that there is a bijection between the set of students and the set of seats

annotations.note: Informal real-world illustration, not a mathematical statement.

annotations.label: Fingerprints

anchor.snippet: Consider a sample of 100 distinct individuals and the corresponding set of their fingerprints

annotations.note: Informal real-world illustration, not a mathematical statement.

annotations.label: Identity function

anchor.snippet: For any set X , the identity function

annotations.note: States `Bijective (@id α)`.

annotations.label: Multiplicative inverse function

anchor.snippet: The multiplicative inverse function gives a bijection of the unit interval

annotations.note: No dedicated bijection on (0,1] given by `x ↦ 1/x` is recorded in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Linear function f(x)=2x+1

anchor.snippet: is bijective, since for each y there is a unique

annotations.note: No named lemma asserts the bijectivity of the concrete affine map `x ↦ 2x+1`.

annotations.label: Arctan bijection

anchor.snippet: given by f ( x ) = arctan( x ) is bijective

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Real.arctan` and its strict monotonicity but no `arctan` bijection onto `(-π/2, π/2)` packaged as an `Equiv`.

annotations.label: Exponential function

anchor.snippet: The exponential function

annotations.note: No named bijection `ℝ ≃ (0, ∞)` via `Real.exp` is recorded; only monotonicity/injectivity lemmas exist.

annotations.label: Squaring function

anchor.snippet: is not bijective: for instance, h (−1) = h (1) = 1

annotations.note: Non-bijectivity of `x ↦ x²` on ℝ is not stated as a named lemma in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Schröder–Bernstein theorem

anchor.snippet: By Schröder–Bernstein theorem , given any two sets X and Y

annotations.note: Two injections give a bijection; cardinal version is `Cardinal.le_antisymm`.

annotations.label: Inverse of a bijection is a bijection

anchor.snippet: the inverse function exists and is also a bijection

annotations.note: `Equiv.symm` produces an inverse equivalence; `Equiv.symm_bijective` shows symm itself is bijective.

annotations.label: Invertible iff bijection

anchor.snippet: A function is invertible if and only if it is a bijection.

annotations.note: Same statement formalized as Bijective ↔ ∃ two-sided inverse.

annotations.label: Bijection (concise condition)

anchor.snippet: for every y in Y there is a unique x in X with y = f ( x ).

annotations.note: Equivalent to `∀ y, ∃! x, f x = y`; `Function.Bijective` captures this.

annotations.label: Composition of bijections

anchor.snippet: of two bijections

annotations.note: `Bijective g → Bijective f → Bijective (g ∘ f)`.

annotations.label: Converse of composition bijective

anchor.snippet: Conversely, if the composition

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Bijective.of_comp_iff` (and its primed dual) but not the exact one-sided converse phrasing about g∘f bijective implying components are bijective.

annotations.label: Finite sets bijection iff same size

anchor.snippet: If X and Y are finite sets , then there exists a bijection between the two sets X and Y if and only if X and Y have the same number of elements.

annotations.note: States `card α = card β ↔ Nonempty (α ≃ β)`.

annotations.label: Countably infinite

anchor.snippet: Any infinite set that has a bijection to the natural numbers is said to be countably infinite.

annotations.note: `Denumerable α` packages a bijection between α and ℕ.

annotations.label: Even integers countably infinite

anchor.snippet: the set of all even integers f(n)=2n is countably infinite

annotations.note: ℤ is denumerable in Mathlib, but the specific bijection ℕ → {even integers} via n ↦ 2n isn't named.

annotations.label: Horizontal/vertical line test

anchor.snippet: is bijective if and only if its graph meets every horizontal and vertical line exactly once.

annotations.note: Pedagogical graph-theoretic characterization not stated as a Mathlib lemma.

annotations.label: Symmetric group of X

anchor.snippet: the bijective functions from X to itself, together with the operation of functional composition

annotations.note: Provides the `Group` instance on `Equiv.Perm α` under composition.

annotations.label: Bijections preserve cardinalities

anchor.snippet: Bijections preserve cardinalities of sets

annotations.note: An equivalence `α ≃ β` yields `#α = #β`.

annotations.label: Finite sets: bijection iff surjection iff injection

anchor.snippet: If X and Y are finite sets with the same cardinality

annotations.note: For self-maps on finite types injectivity, surjectivity and bijectivity coincide; companion `Finite.injective_iff_surjective` exists.

annotations.label: Permutations and total orderings

anchor.snippet: For a finite set S , there is a bijection between the set of possible total orderings of the elements and the set of bijections from S to S .

annotations.note: No explicit bijection between total orderings on a finite set and `Equiv.Perm S` is recorded in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bijections are isomorphisms in Set

anchor.snippet: Bijections are precisely the isomorphisms in the category Set of sets and set functions.

annotations.note: States `IsIso f ↔ Function.Bijective f` in the category `Type`.

annotations.label: Partial bijection

anchor.snippet: The notion of one-to-one correspondence generalizes to partial functions , where they are called partial bijections

annotations.note: `PEquiv α β` is exactly a partial bijection between α and β.

annotations.label: Partial bijection (relation form)

anchor.snippet: Another way of defining the same notion is to say that a partial bijection from A to B is any relation R

annotations.note: Mathlib's `PEquiv` is the functional formulation; the equivalent relation-style characterization is not separately recorded.

annotations.label: One-to-one partial transformation

anchor.snippet: When the partial bijection is on the same set, it is sometimes called a one-to-one partial transformation

annotations.note: The same-set specialization `PEquiv α α` is available but not given a dedicated name.
